title: Hypertensive Emergency: Controlled IV Blood-Pressure Reduction in the ICU
subtitle: A 58-year-old man arrives with BP 226/128, severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion after running out of his antihypertensives. Labs show acute end-organ damage. The nurse must support a CONTROLLED IV blood-pressure reduction and avoid dropping it too fast.

patient.name: Gregory Tan
patient.admitDate: ED then ICU for hypertensive emergency. BP 226/128 with severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion (hypertensive encephalopathy). Ran out of antihypertensives ~1 week ago. End-organ damage on labs. Started on IV nicardipine with an arterial line for continuous monitoring.

nursesNote: Gregory Tan, 58, hypertensive emergency: BP 226/128 with headache, blurred vision, confusion (encephalopathy) and end-organ damage (rising creatinine, troponin, papilledema). Med nonadherence ~1 week. ICU; IV nicardipine titrating with an arterial line. Goal is a controlled reduction, not abrupt normalization.
providerOrders: Admit to ICU; continuous arterial-line BP monitoring and cardiac monitor
IV nicardipine titration; lower MAP by ~10–20% in the first hour
Then target ~160/100 over the next 2–6 hours; avoid abrupt drops
Serial neuro, cardiac, and urine-output assessments
Identify precipitant; resume/optimize oral antihypertensives per orders

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What marks a hypertensive EMERGENCY needing ICU/IV therapy?

opts.text: BP >180/120 WITH acute end-organ damage is a hypertensive emergency
Headache, blurred vision, and confusion suggest hypertensive encephalopathy
Rising creatinine, troponin, and papilledema indicate end-organ damage
This requires ICU admission and IV titratable antihypertensives
Severely high BP with NO end-organ damage is the same as an emergency
An arterial line gives continuous, accurate BP during titration

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: How fast — and how far — should the BP come down?

opts.text: Emergency (end-organ damage) needs IV therapy; urgency uses gradual oral meds
Lower the BP in a CONTROLLED way, not abruptly to normal
Reduce MAP ~10–20% in the first hour, then toward ~160/100 over hours
Dropping BP too fast risks cerebral, coronary, and renal hypoperfusion
The goal is to normalize BP completely within the first hour
Aortic dissection is an exception — lower BP and HR more rapidly

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the management of the hypertensive emergency

opts.text: Recognize end-organ damage and identify a hypertensive emergency
Admit to ICU; establish IV access and continuous BP monitoring
Start the ordered IV titratable antihypertensive
Titrate to a controlled reduction (~10–20% MAP in hour 1); reassess
Watch for too-rapid drop/hypoperfusion and document the response
Use an IV titratable agent with continuous BP monitoring
Reduce MAP ~10–20% in the first hour, not to normal
Place/use an arterial line for beat-to-beat BP
Reassess neuro, cardiac, and renal status frequently
Identify the precipitant and resume oral meds as ordered
Keep the patient on a cardiac monitor in the ICU
Give a rapid IV bolus to drop BP to normal immediately
Use sublingual immediate-release nifedipine to crash the BP
Rely on intermittent cuff readings while titrating a fast IV vasodilator
Treat this emergency with oral meds and discharge home

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure ICU setting, IV access, and continuous BP monitoring
Initiate the ordered IV titratable antihypertensive
Titrate to lower MAP ~10–20% in the first hour
Reassess end-organ status (neuro, chest pain, urine output) and adjust
Transition toward oral therapy, treat the precipitant, and document
Headache and confusion resolve as BP is gradually controlled
Creatinine stabilizes and urine output is adequate
BP reaches ~160/100 over several hours without new symptoms
Patient transitions to oral meds with a follow-up plan
After a rapid BP drop, the patient becomes more confused with chest pain
New focal neuro deficits develop during aggressive titration
Urine output falls and creatinine rises after an abrupt reduction
The patient is discharged on the same regimen that already failed
